chunk_summary: This section discusses protein adequacy in Indian vegetarian diets, emphasizing amino acid complementation, protein quality metrics (DIAAS, PDCAAS), and practical dietary strategies to optimize indispensable amino acid coverage and protein utilization in pediatric nutrition.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of amino acid complementation in vegetarian diets for protein quality.
Learn how to combine cereals and pulses/legumes to improve indispensable amino acid coverage.
Recognize the role of household food processing methods in enhancing protein digestibility and reducing antinutrients.
Apply practical dietary strategies to address growth faltering in children.
key_facts: Protein adequacy depends on total protein quantity, quality (amino acid profile, digestibility), and energy sufficiency.
Cereals (e.g., rice, wheat, millets) are lysine-limited, while pulses/legumes (e.g., dal, soy) are methionine-limited.
Combining cereals with pulses/legumes in the same meal improves amino acid balance.
Adding dairy or eggs (if permitted) further enhances protein quality and energy density.
Household processing methods like fermentation, sprouting, and roasting improve protein digestibility and reduce antinutrients.
For growth faltering, prioritize energy density to meet protein targets within volume limits.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: The ICMR-NIN framework introduces amino-acid-based requirement concepts and modern protein-quality metrics such as DIAAS alongside older PDCAAS approaches.
document_key_topics: Amino acid complementation in vegetarian diets
Dietary strategies for pediatric growth and nutrition
Role of food processing in protein digestibility
